hero.h1: I‑Pace Drive Unit Guide — The Complete UK EV Ownership Guide
hero.subHeadline: The UK's definitive I‑Pace ownership guide — battery degradation data, drive unit failure patterns, real-world range retention, honest repair vs replace economics for Jaguar's first electric SUV. Backed by 40+ real UK I‑Pace enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED].

metrics.ourCall: ⚡ The I‑Pace rule: EV economics are battery-first — drive unit costs are manageable (£6,500–£9,000), but battery degradation is the real ownership story.
[E‑Drive Rear Motor] — earlier rear motor failures resolved by 2021; post-update units show clean record
12V Battery & Charging Module — a frequent no-start complaint, not a drive unit failure
2021+ I‑Pace with Pivi Pro — the infotainment upgrade and battery management improvements make it the definitive generation
⚡ "EV Pioneer" — Jaguar's first and only electric vehicle; stands alone in the range

ownershipVerdict.oneLineVerdict: The I‑Pace is a genuinely compelling EV with a clean reliability record — but buy a 2021+ car for the Pivi Pro upgrade and improved battery management, and treat the 12V battery as a consumable.

generations.h2: Jaguar I‑Pace — One Generation, Three Powertrain Variants
generations.subHeadline: The I‑Pace runs a single generation (X590) but 2021 brought a pivotal hardware/software upgrade. Here's how to choose.

cards.title: Jaguar I‑Pace X590 (2018–2024)
cards.meta: 2018–2024 • Front + Rear Permanent Magnet Synchronous Motors — 90kW each, 294kW total
cards.verdict: The I‑Pace is Jaguar's EV landmark. 2021 cars get Pivi Pro, improved battery management, and a rear motor bearing revision — these are the cars to target. Early cars are still good, but the infotainment alone makes the facelift worthwhile.

engineDatabase.h2: I‑Pace Drive Unit Codes — The Complete Database
engineDatabase.subHeadline: Every electric motor variant fitted to the X590 I‑Pace, with reliability notes and real UK enquiry data.

commonProblems.subHeadline: What actually fails on the I‑Pace — battery, drive units, and the gremlins that come with being Jaguar's first EV.

problems.description: The I‑Pace's most common enquiry — modules failing to sleep correctly, killing the 12V auxiliary battery. Affects all years but can be mitigated with a battery tender.
A documented weak point on earlier cars — bearing wear causes whining noise from the rear drive unit. Revised in 2021 with improved parts.
All EV batteries degrade — the I‑Pace's LG Chem 90kWh pack typically holds 85–95% SOH at 5–7 years. Range loss of 10–20 miles is normal; rapid degradation is a warranty claim.
Touch Pro Duo system is slow to boot, lags during operation, and sometimes crashes. Resolved with the 2021 Pivi Pro upgrade.
Some owners report AC charging faults — usually the on-board charger module failing to negotiate with home wallboxes. Software updates resolve most cases.
The I‑Pace uses liquid cooling for battery and motors — pump failure can cause reduced charging speeds or power limiting. A rare but known failure.

marketIntelligence.h2: Jaguar I‑Pace Market Intelligence — What 40+ UK Owners Told Us in 2025
insights: "Interesting insight": The I‑Pace's most-enquired problem isn't the drive unit — it's the 12V battery. 15+ of the 40 enquiries relate to flat 12V batteries, not motor or battery pack failures. This is a manageable consumable issue, not a fundamental EV flaw. (Data point [JAG-VERIFIED].)

The 2021 model year is a genuine dividing line — rear motor bearing enquiries drop off sharply on post-2021 cars, and infotainment complaints fall to near-zero. If you're buying used, 2021+ is the clear recommendation.

The I‑Pace has no internal combustion engine replacement data because it has no internal combustion engine. What we're seeing instead is a small but growing pattern of rear motor bearing enquiries on early cars — a known wear item that's worth checking before purchase.

editorialPullQuote.title: The One Thing We'd Tell Every I‑Pace Buyer
editorialPullQuote.quote: Buy a 2021+ car if you can. The Pivi Pro infotainment upgrade and rear motor bearing revision transform the ownership experience — and the £7,000–£10,000 premium over a 2018 car is genuinely worth it for the reduced hassle and better residuals. End of Sections 1–7 for Jaguar I‑Pace Ready for "8-14" to continue with Replacement Costs, Engine Evolution, Who Should Buy, Calculator CTA, Trust, FAQ, and Closing Action Cards. JAGUAR I-PACE — SECTIONS 8-14

replacementCosts.h2: Jaguar I‑Pace Drive Unit Replacement Costs — By Component
replacementCosts.subHeadline: Real UK pricing for I‑Pace drive unit and battery components — supply-only, with labour guidance. All figures are [JAG-QUOTE] from specialist quotes.
replacementCosts.figuresNote: Labour estimate: Fitting an I‑Pace drive unit typically takes 8–14 hours per unit (10–14 hrs for rear, 8–12 hrs for front) at £65–£95/hr (independent) or £110–£160/hr (London/SE dealer) [JAG-QUOTE]. Front and rear drive units are independent — failure of one does not necessarily mean the other needs replacement. The I‑Pace rule — the squeeze is the story: Unlike the rest of the Jaguar range, the I‑Pace has no internal combustion engine to worry about. A complete dual-drive-unit replacement at £6,500–£9,000 sits at roughly 20–40% of a typical £18,000–£45,000 I‑Pace value — well below our 60% scrap threshold for Jaguar. But the real economics question isn't the drive unit — it's the battery. A battery replacement (not covered in this data) can exceed £15,000–£20,000, which pushes even a £35,000 car toward the threshold. The smart move? Buy a 2021+ car with a healthy battery health certificate and treat the 12V battery as a normal consumable. [THIRD-PARTY] — don't walk away from a drive unit repair; walk away from a car with poor battery health.

engineEvolution.h2: How the I‑Pace Drive Unit Range Evolved — 2018 to Today

whoShouldBuy.h2: Who Should Buy a Jaguar I‑Pace?

profiles.verdict: The I‑Pace has a spacious cabin and 505L boot — enough for a family of four. The ride is comfortable, but rear headroom is limited for tall passengers. A 2021+ car with Pivi Pro is the family choice — better tech for long journeys.

230–290 miles real-world range (depending on 2018 vs 2021) makes the I‑Pace a credible commuter. The 2021+ car's improved battery management means 20+ more miles per charge than early cars. If you can charge at home, it's a brilliant daily driver.

A 2018–2020 car from £18k–£25k is excellent value, but factor in the battery health check (£150–£300) and 12V battery replacement (£200–£400) as immediate costs. The budget sweet spot is a 2019–2020 car with verified >88% SOH.

0–60 in 4.5 seconds and 394bhp across all years — the I‑Pace is genuinely quick. The 2021+ car's thermal management means you can enjoy that performance for longer without overheating. The Sportier EV choice over a Tesla Model Y.

The I‑Pace is a gateway car — your first premium EV, your first Jaguar, or both. It's more engaging to drive than a Tesla, more prestigious than a Kia EV6, and the 2021 update fixes the early teething issues. Buy a 2021+ car and you'll have a genuinely good ownership experience.

calculatorCta.h2: Can I Repair My I‑Pace Drive Unit — or Should I Replace It?
calculatorCta.intro: H3: The I‑Pace's most common failure — the 12V battery — is a simple £200–£400 fix. But if you're hearing a whine from the rear motor or your range has dropped significantly, it's time for a proper diagnosis.

signals.title: Built on Real Data
signals.text: Every reliability rating and cost range comes from genuine UK quote and enquiry data — 40+ I‑Pace enquiries in 2025 [JAG-VERIFIED], not manufacturer marketing or forum guesswork.

signals.title: We Tell You When Not to Repair
signals.text: Where replacement cost exceeds what the car is worth, we say so. For the I‑Pace, drive unit replacement comfortably passes our 60% threshold — but battery replacement is a different story. We tell you the truth, not the sale.

signals.title: Vetted Jaguar Specialists
signals.text: 15+ vetted Jaguar specialists in our network, including EV-trained technicians who understand the I‑Pace's unique drive unit and battery management systems. No generalists — just Jaguar people.
